title: Two Sum
description: Given an array of integers and a target value, determine if there are any two integers in the array whose sum equals the given value. Return true if the sum exists, false otherwise. You may not use the same element twice, and the answer can be returned in any order.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Set Rows and Columns to Zero
description: Given a two-dimensional array (matrix), if any element is zero, set its entire row and column to zero.
approach: Zeros at positions (0,0) and (1,2) cause their respective rows and columns to be set to zero.

title: Add Two Numbers Represented by Linked Lists
description: Given the head pointers of two linked lists where each linked list represents an integer number (each node is a digit, with the first node as the least significant digit), add the numbers and return the resulting linked list.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Copy Linked List with Arbitrary Pointer
description: Given a linked list where each node has two pointers—next (regular pointer) and arbitrary_pointer (can point to any node in the list)—create a deep copy of the linked list. The deep copy should be independent, meaning operations on the original list (inserting, modifying, removing) should not affect the copied list.
examples: Input: Linked list with nodes [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] and arbitrary pointers (e.g., 1->3, 2->1, 3->5, 4->3, 5->2)Output: A new linked list with the same structure and arbitrary pointer connections.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Level Order Traversal of Binary Tree
description: Given the root of a binary tree, display the node values at each level (level-order traversal). Return the result as a list of lists, where each inner list contains the node values at a particular level.
examples: Input: Binary tree with root = 5, left = 9, right = 12, right.left = 25, right.right = 7Output: [[5], [9, 12], [25, 7]]
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Connect Sibling Pointers in Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree, connect the sibling pointer of each node to the next node at the same level. The last node in each level should point to the first node of the next level.
examples: Input: Binary tree with nodes [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]Output: Nodes connected such that 1->2, 2->3, 3->4, 4->5, 5->null
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Find Missing Number in Array
description: Given an array of positive numbers from 1 to n, where all numbers except one (x) are present, find the missing number x. The array is unsorted.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Merge Two Sorted Linked Lists
description: Write a function that takes two sorted linked lists and merges them into a single sorted linked list by splicing their nodes together.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Check if Binary Tree is Symmetric
description: Given a binary tree, determine if it is a mirror image of itself (symmetric). Return true if symmetric, false otherwise.
examples: Input: Tree with root = 1, left = 2, right = 2, left.left = 3, left.right = 4, right.left = 4, right.right = 3Output: true
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Reverse Words in a Sentence
description: Reverse the order of words in a given sentence.
examples: Input: "sphinx of black quartz judge my vow"Output: "vow my judge quartz black of sphinx"
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: String Segmentation
description: Given a dictionary of words and a large input string, determine if the input string can be completely segmented into words from the dictionary.
examples: Input: s = "applepenapple", dictionary = ["apple", "pen"]Output: true (because "applepenapple" can be segmented as "apple pen apple")
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Find Maximum Single Sell Profit
description: Given a list of daily stock prices (integers), return the buy and sell prices that maximize the profit from a single buy/sell transaction. If no profit is possible, minimize the loss.
examples: Input: prices = [7, 1, 5, 3, 6, 4]Output: Buy at 1, sell at 6 (profit = 5)
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Length of Longest Subsequence (Increasing then Decreasing)
description: Given a one-dimensional integer array of length n, find the length of the longest subsequence that increases before decreasing.
examples: Input: arr = [1, 2, 3, 2, 1]Output: 5 (the entire array is a valid subsequence)
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Longest Path in Matrix
description: Given an n*n matrix where all numbers are distinct, find the longest path starting from any cell such that all cells along the path increase in order by 1.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Find All Sum Combinations
description: Given a positive integer target, print all possible combinations of positive integers that sum up to the target.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Find Kth Permutation
description: Given a set of n variables, find the kth permutation of those variables.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Regular Expression Matching
description: Given a text and a pattern, determine if the pattern matches the text completely using regular expression matching. The pattern contains only two operators: . (matches any single character) and * (the preceding character may appear zero or more times).
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Rat in a Maze
description: A rat is placed at position (0,0) in a square n*n matrix. Find all possible paths to reach the destination (n-1, n-1). The rat can move vertically or horizontally, only through cells with value 1 (traversable), and cannot visit a cell more than once. Cells with value 0 are impassable.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Solve the Sudoku
description: Given a 9*9 square matrix representing an incomplete Sudoku puzzle, return true if the puzzle can be completed and provide the completed grid. Otherwise, return false.
examples: Input: grid = [[5,3,.,.,7,.,.,.,.], ...] (partial Sudoku grid)Output: true (with completed grid) or false
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Clone a Directed Graph
description: Given the root node of a directed graph, create a deep copy of the graph with the same vertices and edges.
examples: Input: Graph with nodes [1, 2, 3] and edges [1->2, 2->3, 3->1]Output: A new graph with identical structure
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Breadth-First Traversal of a Graph
description: Starting from a source node, traverse a given graph breadthwise to find the distance between the source node and node n.
examples: Input: Graph with nodes [1, 2, 3, 4], edges [1->2, 2->3, 3->4], source = 1, target = 4Output: 3 (distance from 1 to 4)
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Check Path from Source to Destination
description: Given an n*n graph where each cell contains a value (0 = source, 1 = wall, 2 = blank, 3 = destination), determine if there is a path from the source to the destination. The graph can be traversed horizontally and vertically.
examples: Input: graph = [[0, 2, 2], [1, 1, 2], [2, 2, 3]]Output: true (path exists from (0,0) to (2,2))
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Find Closest Meeting Point
description: Given n people on a square grid, find the point that requires the least total distance for all people to meet at that point.
examples: Input: grid = 5x5 with people at positions [(0,0), (1,1), (2,2)]Output: (1,1) (minimizes total Manhattan distance)
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.
